topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropping onto a wooden floor. My breath hitched, a shallow, unsatisfying movement in my chest. The subject line read simply: "Regarding your recent submission." My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly. It was a notification that twisted a familiar knot of unease in my stomach. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes. My carefully crafted piece, the one I poured weeks of sleepless nights into, was credited to a completely different author. A stranger’s name adorned the byline, and my own contribution was nowhere to be found. A cold, creeping sensation began to spread from my fingertips up my arms, leaving me feeling strangely numb and heavy, as if the air itself had thickened around me. I leaned back in my worn chair, the springs protesting beneath my weight, and stared blankly at the beige wall, the silence of my apartment amplifying the hollow echo of disappointment.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred into meaningless shapes. When I finally read the confirmation, a strange, sinking feeling settled deep in my core. It was a quiet deflation, like a balloon slowly losing its air. I reread the text three times, each repetition bringing a fresh wave of weary resignation. The person whose name was attached to my work was someone I vaguely recognized, a name that felt distant and untouchable. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the linoleum sounding unnaturally loud in the quiet room. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the muted grey of the city below. Everything seemed muted, drained of color, mirroring the internal landscape I was currently inhabiting—flat, unresponsive, and utterly depleted.

[story 3]

The realization hit me not with a crash, but with a slow, agonizing seep, like water finding cracks in dry earth. I sat at my desk, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead seeming to mock my stillness. My hands, usually steady when typing, began to shake almost imperceptibly. I picked up the printed acceptance letter, tracing the unfamiliar signature with a fingertip. A profound sense of unfairness washed over me, a heavy cloak settling over my shoulders. I let the paper slip from my grasp, watching it flutter down onto the desk blotter. A low, shaky sigh escaped my lips, a sound devoid of any real feeling, just the residue of exhaustion. I didn't move to delete the submission; instead, I just sat there, the weight of the situation pressing down, making the very act of breathing feel like a deliberate, burdensome effort.
